title: Reading Chess Notation
summary: Decode any chess move in algebraic notation

steps.title: The Coordinate System
steps.text:
Chess has its own language — **algebraic notation** — and once you know it, you can read any game from history, follow any commentary, and study any book.

Every square on the board has a unique name made of a **letter** and a **number**.

## How squares are named

- **Files** (the columns going up the board) are letters **a–h**, left to right from White's point of view.
- **Ranks** (the rows) are numbers **1–8**, starting from White's first row.
- Combine the two and every square is uniquely named — *file first, then rank*.

So White's king starts on **e1**, his queen on **d1**, and the most famous square in chess — the one White's pawn often grabs first — is **e4**.

Quick orienteer: a1 is the bottom-left corner from White's side; h8 is the top-right.

> If you can name every square without thinking, you're already ahead of most casual players.

steps.title: Piece Letters
steps.text:
Each piece (except the pawn) gets a single capital letter.

## The piece alphabet

- **K** — King
- **Q** — Queen
- **R** — Rook
- **B** — Bishop
- **N** — Knight (`N` because `K` is already King)
- *(pawn — no letter at all)*

A move is just **piece letter + destination square**. So {{Nf3}} means *knight to f3*. Pawn moves skip the letter — {{e4}} simply means *the pawn that can legally reach e4*.

## Captures

- An **x** between piece and square marks a capture: {{Nxe5}} = *knight captures on e5*.
- A **pawn capture** uses the pawn's starting file: {{exd5}} = *the e-pawn captures on d5*.

> Pawns are humble: no letter, but they get to use their file when they capture.

steps.title: Special Symbols
steps.text:
A handful of extra marks let notation describe everything that can happen on the board.

## The full toolkit

- **+** — check. Example: {{Bb5+}}.
- **#** — checkmate. Example: {{Qh7#}}.
- **O-O** — kingside (short) castling.
- **O-O-O** — queenside (long) castling.
- **=Q** — promotion (any piece). Example: {{e8=Q}} promotes the e-pawn to a queen on e8.
- **!** and **?** — annotator's marks for *good* and *dubious* moves; **!!** brilliant, **??** blunder.

## Disambiguation

When two same-color pieces could move to the same square, you add either the file or rank of the moving piece:

- {{Nbd2}} — the *b-file* knight moves to d2 (when the other knight could too).
- {{R1e1}} — the rook on the *1st rank* moves to e1 (when both rooks share the e-file).

> Once you can read notation, you can read every chess book in history.

steps.prompt: Open with the king's pawn — play **{{e4}}**, the most popular first move in chess.
steps.hint: No piece letter means it's a pawn. Push the e-pawn two squares to e4.

steps.prompt: Now bring out a knight with **{{Nf3}}** — attacking e5 and developing toward the center.
steps.hint: Capital N means knight. From g1, it lands on f3 in an L-shape.
